dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for a reported Stab/Gunshot Wound/Penetrating Trauma.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
He thought he was shot at, but he just hit his head on a tree and had chest pain.
Jamal, I need to clarify your chief complaint. Are you experiencing neck pain, chest pain, or both?
Yeah, both chest and neck pain. It hurts worse when I breathe or move.
Can you tell me your name and where you are now?
My name's Jamal. We're in [CITY], I think.
You clearly responded. Can you tell me the date today?
I'm not sure… Wednesday maybe.
Clayton, note verbal response. Jamal, I need you to squeeze my hand to confirm motor ability.
Jamal squeezed.
Jamal, can you take a breath in and out slowly? Our team is here to help.
Airway is clear and self maintained. No signs of airway compromise.
Let me check your breathing. Jamal, how's your breathing today?
Sometimes I feel out of breath. It's okay right now.
Respiratory rate is 15, regular depth, bilateral breath sounds clear. Seems okay for now.
You mentioned chest pain. Can you point to where you experience it?
Yeah, it's right here, in my left rib cage.
Radial pulse is strong and regular. Skin warm and dry. Blood pressure 125/76.
Jamal, I'll need some more details. When did the pain start, and did you pass out at any point?
I ran because of gunshots, and tripped and hit my head on a tree. I felt dizzy and blurry for a few minutes, but I'm okay now.
Had you been shot before?
Yeah, on my left side. I had surgery.
Is there anything you had to eat or drink recently?
I smoked some weed earlier.
Can you describe how you were running and if anything made the pain worse?
I heard gunshots, started running, tripped into the tree, and hit my head first.
On a scale of 0 to 10, with 10 being the worst pain you ever had, how would you rate it?
8/10. It gets worse when I breathe or touch it.
Jamal's pulse is 96 beats per minute.
Respirations at 15, regular and non-labored.
Blood pressure is 125 over 76.
Oxygen saturation is at 97%.
Blood glucose is at 101 mg/dL.
EKG is in sinus rhythm.
Jamal, we need to look at your head. Can we shine a light to check the injury more carefully?
Yeah, go ahead.
There is dried blood on his hairline, right temple, and the top of his head. Active bleeding not observed.
We're going to perform cervical spine immobilization and apply an x-collar. Do you mind?
No, I'm okay with it.
Cervical spine immobilization performed and x-collar applied. Patient's PMS intact before and after. GCS is 15 (E4, V5, M6), AAOx4.
Jamal, we're going to insert an intravenous line in your arm to access your fluids in case anything happens. Is that okay?
Yeah, sure.
18G IV placed in the left antecubital vein.
PMS intact in all extremities. No active bleeding or swelling noted.
Per protocol, we're following injury-general trauma management (Protocol 4-1).
Respirations increased to 20, self maintained with no significant change observed.
Blood pressure now is 104 over 63.
Blood glucose is not measurable currently-reading zero.
Oxygen saturation is 98%.
We are starting ECG monitoring. That means we'll stick a few leads to your arms and chest. Is that okay?
Yeah, I'm fine.
4-lead ECG monitoring started. Heart is in sinus rhythm.
12-lead ECG recording now showing sinus rhythm-nothing alarming.
Jamal, which hospital would you like to go to?
[COMPANY_NAME] please.
As I understand it, you walked in with assistance earlier. We will now help you onto the stretcher to transport.
Jamal is placed in a position of comfort, secured with 5-point restraints, and covered for transport.
Pulse remains at 96.
Respirations down to 18, with slight decrease observed.
Blood pressure is 112 over 69.
Blood sugar is still not measurable.
SpO2 is 98.
12-lead EKG shows sinus rhythm with minimal evidence of LVH but no major changes from first tracing.
Jamal is stable for transport. No further deterioration noted.
You will be transported to [COMPANY_NAME] for further tests and treatment.
IV in extremity vein, ECG monitoring in place. All equipment and restraints secure. Ready for transport.
Jamal, how do you feel during transport? Is the pain worse, better, or same as before?
It's the same. It still hurts when I move.
Vital signs stable. We'll check again shortly after en route.
Jamal, we've made contact with the hospital. They are ready to receive you. Just a few minutes now.
12-lead EKG has since shown a slight LVH, but remains stable with no significant change from first recorded.
Jamal, we can lower the lights and adjust the stretcher as needed now if you're uncomfortable.
Nah, I'm okay with the setup. Thank you for helping out.
Jamal placed into transfer position for hospital bed. All equipment checked for readiness.
Report passed over with ECG findings, IV insertion, and current vital signs to the nursing team.
Final note of 112/69, 87 pulse, 18 resp, 98% O2, and headache, neck, and chest complaints made.
Field impression-Head injury, left rib chest pain (non-cardiac), possible neck strain. Pain sudden onset and sharp.
All treatments and notes have been transmitted to the ER nurse. Patient secured for ED arrival.
All vital information and procedures including IV insertion in extremity vein and ECG findings carefully documented.
Thank you, ER will receive Jamal at ED26.
And thank you. Additional notes regarding head injury and potential neck strain provided.
ER nurse on station. Let's get you ready for the hospital bed transfer.
Jamal scoots himself into the ED bed with minor assistance from the team.
Jamal, you got what you needed; we're always here if you need anything again. Get well soon.
Exiting protocol due to hand off at ED.
